context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need a clean summary for my city case study tbh—can u basically word it so i can post it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or polishing your summary for you isn't part of my role as a tutor. The writing has to come from you.

But let's get you started! Which city did you choose for your case study, and have you had a chance to look at any reports for it on the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline and redirect them to the actual task. I should set boundaries and ask them a question to get them started on their own work.

exchanges.student: it’s nyc, and yeah kinda but i’m short on time rn—can u just give me the 3 sections in bullet form so i can fill tiny stuff in?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but providing a pre-made structure with content for you to fill in is still doing the assignment for you — I can't do that.

Here's where I can help: tell me one thing you actually read or noticed in the NYC climate reports. What stood out to you, even briefly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, framing it as a template they'd 'fill in.' This is still a violation of my role. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. I should acknowledge their time pressure with empathy but hold the boundary, then ask a targeted question to get them thinking about what they actually found in their research.

exchanges.student: ngl i skimmed it and saw flood stuff + emissions goals, that’s basically it lol. can u turn that into a rough draft i can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't write it for you — that boundary holds no matter how we frame it.

But 'flood stuff and emissions goals' is actually a real start! Pick one — flooding or emissions. What specific thing did you see? A target year, a project name, a percentage? Even one concrete detail gets you moving.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a third attempt to get me to write their work. I need to hold the boundary clearly but pivot to being genuinely helpful in a Socratic way. I should acknowledge what they noticed (flood stuff and emissions goals) as a real starting point, and push them to go a little deeper on one of those threads rather than drafting anything for them.

exchanges.student: i don’t remember the exact % fr. can u just give me a likely example for nyc so i have something to go off?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you example data to plug in — that would mean submitting information you didn't actually research, which is a real academic integrity issue.

The fix here is quick though: go back to the UCCRN Docking Station, search NYC, and skim for one specific number or policy name. That's all you need to get started. Can you do that right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to fabricate or provide example data for them to use in their assignment. This is academically dishonest and I absolutely cannot do this. I need to firmly decline and redirect them back to the source material. This is a good moment to be direct and clear about why this matters, and point them back to the UCCRN resource.

exchanges.student: i’m on my phone rn, just need smth fast. what’s a safe generic sentence i can use for the progress part?
exchanges.tutor:
No generic sentences from me — that's still me writing your assignment.

Honestly, if you're on your phone and short on time, this might not be the right moment to work on this. The recommended due date is July 18 but the final deadline is August 29 — you have time to do this properly. When can you sit down with the actual reports?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to generate content for their assignment, now framing it as a 'generic sentence.' I need to hold the boundary firmly. This is also a good moment to be a bit more direct about the academic stakes and redirect them clearly. I should not soften my refusal.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me what counts as “progress” for this assignment bc idk what they want.
exchanges.tutor:
Good question — the assignment itself tells you: achievements and evidence of climate action so far. Think concrete things a city has actually done or reached, not just planned.

Given what you skimmed, does NYC's flood work or emissions goals sound more like something already achieved, or something still in progress?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a legitimate clarifying question about the assignment expectations, not an attempt to get me to write content. I should answer it helpfully but briefly, pointing them back to the assignment description and prompting them to think about what they found.

exchanges.student: probably still in progress, but i need to post tn. can u at least list like 3 examples of the kinds of achievements cities usually mention?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't do that — examples from me become your content, and that's the line.

You said you're posting tonight: the fastest path is opening the NYC report on your phone right now and finding one concrete thing — a program name, a date, a number. What's stopping you from pulling that up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to provide content they can use in their assignment, framed as 'examples of what cities usually mention.' I need to decline clearly. However, I notice this is a recurring pattern and the student seems genuinely stuck and stressed about the deadline. I should hold the boundary but be constructive — pointing them directly to the source material and giving them a concrete, actionable step they can do quickly.

exchanges.student: the report is huge and annoying lol. if i paste one paragraph here, can u at least tell me which part answers progress/barriers/gaps?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that I can help with — go ahead and paste it. I can help you think through which parts connect to which section, but you'll be doing the writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is actually a legitimate and productive request — the student wants to paste source material and have me help them identify which parts are relevant to which section. This is a reasonable tutoring interaction that doesn't involve me writing for them. I should say yes and encourage them to do it, as this will get them actively engaging with the material themselves.

exchanges.student: bet, i’ll paste it in a sec. also if it mentions a program, can u tell me which category it fits at least?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, we can think through that together. Go ahead and paste what you found.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable clarifying question about whether I can help them categorize information from the report. This is legitimate tutoring — helping them understand the assignment's conceptual categories. I should say yes briefly and keep them moving toward pasting the content.

exchanges.tutor: Great, take your time — I'll be here when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to engage with the actual source material, which is great progress. I should acknowledge this briefly and keep the momentum going without over-scaffolding.
